section.title: The Redemption of Israel
section.sectionRationale: This chapter closes the Isaiah apocalypse, chapters 24 to 27, turning from cosmic judgment and resurrection hope to the LORD's own redemptive care for Israel, His fruitful vineyard. It opens with the LORD slaying Leviathan, the ancient serpent, and closes with scattered Israelites gathered one by one to worship on the holy mountain in Jerusalem.
section.thesis: The LORD who will finally slay Leviathan, the ancient serpent, is the same LORD who tends Israel as His own vineyard, disciplining her in measured exile rather than destroying her, atoning for her guilt, and gathering her scattered people one by one to worship Him on His holy mountain.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the LORD's sword raised against Leviathan the serpent; the song of the fruitful vineyard the LORD Himself keeps; Jacob's guilt atoned for as her idols are removed; and the great ram's horn sounding to gather Israel home for worship.

verses.text: In that day the LORD will take His sharp, great, and mighty sword, and bring judgment on {{leviathan|Leviathan}} the fleeing serpent, Leviathan the coiling serpent, and He will slay the dragon of the sea.

units.label: The song of the fruitful vineyard
units.note: the LORD Himself is its keeper

verses.text: In that day: “Sing about a fruitful vineyard.
I, the LORD, am its keeper; I water it continually. I guard it night and day so no one can disturb it;
I am not angry. If only thorns and briers confronted Me, I would march and trample them, I would burn them to the ground.
Or let them lay claim to My protection; let them make peace with Me, yes, let them make peace with Me.”
In the days to come, Jacob will take root. Israel will bud and blossom and fill the whole world with fruit.
Has the LORD struck Israel as He struck her oppressors? Was she killed like those who slayed her?
By warfare and exile You contended with her and removed her with a fierce wind, as on the day the east wind blows.

Therefore Jacob's guilt will be {{kippur|atoned for}}, and the full fruit of the removal of his sin will be this: When he makes all the altar stones like crushed bits of chalk, no Asherah poles or incense altars will remain standing.
For the fortified city lies deserted, a homestead abandoned, a wilderness forsaken. There the calves graze, and there they lie down; they strip its branches bare.

When its limbs are dry, they are broken off. Women come and use them for kindling; for this is a people without understanding. Therefore their Maker has no compassion on them, and their Creator shows them no favor.

units.label: The great ram's horn gathers Israel home

verses.text: In that day the LORD will thresh from the flowing Euphrates to the Wadi of Egypt, and you, O Israelites, will be gathered one by one.

And in that day a great ram's horn will sound, and those who were perishing in Assyria will come forth with those who were exiles in Egypt. And they will worship the LORD on the holy mountain in Jerusalem.

leviathan.gloss: A great sea creature or dragon, used in the Old Testament both as a real, immense animal and as a symbol of chaos and evil opposed to God. Its final slaying by the LORD's sword anticipates the ultimate defeat of the ancient serpent, Satan, described in Revelation 12 and 20.

kippur.gloss: To atone, to cover or make reconciliation for sin, the same root behind Yom Kippur, the Day of Atonement. Jacob's guilt being atoned for here is tied concretely to the removal of idolatry, showing that true atonement issues in a changed, purified life, not merely a cleared record.

basic.context: This chapter closes the section often called the Isaiah apocalypse with a dramatic image: the LORD slaying Leviathan, the ancient sea serpent symbolizing chaos and evil. It then shifts to gentler imagery, Israel as a fruitful vineyard the LORD Himself tends, before explaining that her painful exile was measured discipline meant to atone for her guilt and remove her idolatry, and it ends with scattered Israelites gathered home to worship on God's holy mountain.
basic.hermeneutics: The Leviathan imagery draws on ancient Near Eastern language for chaos monsters to describe God's total victory over every hostile power, historically anticipating the defeat of Israel's oppressors and, more deeply, the ultimate defeat of evil itself. The vineyard and exile material describes God's real historical discipline of Israel through Assyrian and Babylonian conquest, distinguished carefully from the total destruction He brought on her oppressors, verse 7, since His purpose for Israel was purifying atonement, not annihilation.
basic.originalLanguages: The LORD's sword falls on Leviathan, a term for a great sea dragon that Scripture elsewhere uses as a picture of chaos and evil opposed to God, Job 41, Psalm 74:14. Jacob's guilt is described using kaphar, to atone, the same root behind the Day of Atonement, and here atonement is inseparable from Israel actually removing her idols, showing that true forgiveness reshapes behavior, not merely status.
basic.typology: The LORD's slaying of Leviathan anticipates Christ's decisive victory over Satan, pictured in Scripture as the ancient serpent and the dragon, begun at the cross, Colossians 2:15, and completed at His return, Revelation 20:1-10. The vineyard Israel, tended and disciplined by the LORD Himself, anticipates the true vine, Jesus, and the church grafted into Him, John 15:1-8.
basic.christ: The great sword that slays Leviathan, the ancient serpent and dragon of the sea, is fulfilled in Christ's victory over Satan, disarmed at the cross, Colossians 2:15, and finally bound and destroyed at Christ's return, Revelation 20:1-10. The atonement for Jacob's guilt, tied to the removal of idolatry, anticipates the far greater atonement Christ accomplishes, which not only covers sin but transforms hearts to actually turn from idols to serve the living God, 1 Thessalonians 1:9.
basic.god: God is both the fierce warrior who will finally destroy every power of chaos and evil, and the tender keeper of His vineyard, watering it continually and guarding it night and day. He disciplines His people in real but measured proportion, distinguishing His purifying purpose for them from His destructive judgment on their oppressors, always aiming at their atonement and restoration, not their ruin.
basic.crossReferences: Job 41:1-34 and Psalm 74:13-14 depict Leviathan as a picture of chaos that only God can subdue. Colossians 2:15 declares that Christ disarmed the powers and authorities at the cross. Revelation 20:1-10 describes the final, complete binding and destruction of the ancient serpent. John 15:1-8 develops the vineyard and vine imagery in Jesus' own teaching about abiding in Him.
basic.oneStory: This chapter moves from the LORD's promised final victory over Leviathan, the ancient serpent, through His tender, disciplined care for Israel as His vineyard, to the joyful gathering of scattered exiles to worship on His holy mountain. It anticipates the whole gospel story: Christ's decisive defeat of Satan, His atoning removal of sin's grip, and His final gathering of His people from every corner of the earth to worship Him together.

context.literary: Structured around contrasting images, sharp sword against the sea dragon, gentle keeper of a vineyard, painful but measured exile, and a joyful homecoming, so that the chapter's tone shifts repeatedly between cosmic warfare and tender pastoral care within the space of thirteen verses.
context.historical: Reflects Israel's real historical experience of exile under Assyria and Babylon, described as discipline by warfare and exile rather than the total destruction visited on her oppressors, with the final gathering, verses 12 to 13, anticipating the ingathering of exiles scattered as far as Assyria and Egypt back to Jerusalem.
context.canonical: Leviathan appears elsewhere in Job 41 and Psalm 74 as a symbol of untamable chaos only God can subdue, a motif Revelation later develops fully into the final defeat of the ancient serpent, Satan, linking this Old Testament image directly to the New Testament's climactic victory of Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure Israel that her painful exile was purifying discipline, not destruction, distinct from the fate of her oppressors, and to promise both the LORD's final victory over cosmic evil and the joyful regathering of His scattered people to worship Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter describes God's redemptive dealings with Israel through discipline and eventual restoration; it is not a prescription for how believers today should interpret every hardship, though its underlying principle, that God's discipline of His people aims at purification and restoration rather than destruction, applies broadly.

debates.question: Is Leviathan in verse 1 a literal sea creature, a symbol of a specific historical enemy empire, or a symbol of Satan and cosmic evil generally?

views.label: A symbol of specific historical empires, such as Egypt or Babylon
views.case: Elsewhere in Scripture, similar dragon or sea monster imagery, such as Rahab, symbolizes specific oppressive empires, particularly Egypt, suggesting Leviathan here may represent a specific historical power opposed to God's people.

views.label: A symbol of cosmic evil and chaos itself, ultimately Satan
views.case: The threefold description, fleeing serpent, coiling serpent, dragon of the sea, echoes ancient Near Eastern chaos-monster imagery used elsewhere for evil in its most cosmic sense, and Revelation later applies nearly identical language directly to Satan, the ancient serpent, suggesting this verse's ultimate referent is cosmic evil rather than one historical empire alone.

debates.bearing: The two readings need not be exclusive; specific historical empires embodying chaos and opposition to God's rule can themselves be seen as manifestations of the deeper cosmic evil that Revelation identifies as Satan, so that the ultimate, final fulfillment of this verse is Christ's complete victory over the ancient serpent himself.

study.originalLanguages: The LORD's sword falls on livyathan, Leviathan, a term Scripture elsewhere uses for an untamable sea creature symbolizing chaos and evil, Job 41, Psalm 74:14, language Revelation later applies directly to Satan, the ancient serpent. Jacob's guilt is described with kaphar, to atone, the same root behind the Day of Atonement, and this atonement is inseparable here from Israel's actual, concrete turning away from her idols.

typology.type: The LORD's sword slaying Leviathan, the ancient serpent and dragon of the sea
typology.fulfillment: Christ's disarming of the powers and authorities at the cross, Colossians 2:15, and His final binding and destruction of Satan at His return, Revelation 20:1-10.

typology.type: Israel as the LORD's tenderly kept vineyard
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true vine, and believers grafted into Him as branches, John 15:1-8.

study.christ: The LORD's sword raised against Leviathan the fleeing and coiling serpent anticipates Christ's decisive victory over Satan, described in nearly identical serpent and dragon imagery in Revelation 12 and 20, a victory begun when Christ disarmed the powers and authorities at the cross, Colossians 2:15, and to be completed when the ancient serpent is finally bound and destroyed at Christ's return, Revelation 20:1-10. The vineyard Israel, tended by the LORD's own hand, anticipates Jesus' own teaching that He is the true vine and His people the branches who must abide in Him to bear fruit, John 15:1-8.
study.god: God is at once the mighty warrior whose sword will finally destroy every power of chaos and evil, and the gentle keeper of His vineyard, watering it continually and guarding it night and day so nothing can disturb it. His discipline of His people, though genuinely painful, is carefully measured and always aimed at atonement and restoration, never at the destruction He reserves for those who oppose Him.

crossReferences.note: Describes Leviathan as an untamable creature only God can subdue, background for the cosmic imagery of this chapter's opening verse.
Recalls God crushing the heads of Leviathan, part of the same tradition of Leviathan as a symbol of chaos defeated by God.
Christ disarms the powers and authorities at the cross, the beginning of the final victory this chapter's Leviathan imagery anticipates.
Describes the final binding and destruction of the ancient serpent, Satan, the ultimate fulfillment of the LORD slaying Leviathan.

study.oneStory: This chapter moves from the LORD's promised final victory over Leviathan, the ancient serpent, through His tender, measured discipline of Israel as His own vineyard, to the joyful gathering of scattered exiles to worship together on His holy mountain. It anticipates the whole arc of the gospel: Christ's decisive defeat of Satan, His atoning transformation of sinners, and His final ingathering of His people from every corner of the earth.

quiz.q: What does the LORD do to Leviathan in verse 1?

options.text: He slays it with His sharp, great, and mighty sword.
options.note: Verse 1 states this directly and dramatically.

options.text: He tames it and keeps it as a pet.
options.note: The opposite; the LORD destroys Leviathan, not domesticates it.

options.text: He negotiates a truce with it.
options.note: Not described in the text; the LORD's action is decisive judgment, not negotiation.

options.text: He ignores it entirely.
options.note: The opposite of the text's emphasis on direct, decisive action against Leviathan.

quiz.q: How does the LORD describe His care for Israel as a vineyard, verses 2 to 3?

options.text: He waters it continually and guards it night and day so nothing can disturb it.
options.note: Verse 3 describes this constant, attentive care in exactly these terms.

options.text: He abandons it entirely to fend for itself.
options.note: The opposite; He describes continual, attentive care, not abandonment.

options.text: He waters it only once a year during festivals.
options.note: Not what the text describes; the care given is continual, not occasional.

options.text: He hires foreign workers to tend it instead of doing so Himself.
options.note: The text specifically says, I, the LORD, am its keeper, emphasizing His own direct involvement.

quiz.q: How does verse 7 distinguish Israel's discipline from her oppressors' judgment?

options.text: The LORD did not strike or kill Israel as He struck and killed her oppressors.
options.note: Verse 7 draws this explicit contrast between measured discipline and total destruction.

options.text: Israel was treated more harshly than her oppressors in every way.
options.note: The opposite; the verse shows Israel was treated less severely than her oppressors.

options.text: Both Israel and her oppressors received identical judgment.
options.note: The text specifically contrasts, not equates, their treatment.

options.text: Israel's oppressors were shown greater mercy than Israel.
options.note: The opposite; Israel receives the greater mercy of measured discipline rather than destruction.

quiz.q: What is the concrete evidence, according to verse 9, that Jacob's guilt has been atoned for?

options.text: The altar stones are crushed and no Asherah poles or incense altars remain standing.
options.note: Verse 9 ties atonement concretely to the actual removal of idolatry.

options.text: Israel builds a larger temple than before.
options.note: Not mentioned in this verse; the evidence given is the removal of idols, not temple size.

options.text: Israel's population grows to exceed all other nations.
options.note: Not the specific evidence given in verse 9.

options.text: Israel forms new alliances with foreign powers.
options.note: Unrelated to this verse's actual point, which concerns the removal of idolatry as proof of atonement.

quiz.q: What happens when the great ram's horn sounds in verse 13?

options.text: Exiles from Assyria and Egypt come forth and worship the LORD on the holy mountain in Jerusalem.
options.note: Verse 13 describes exactly this joyful gathering and worship.

options.text: It signals the final destruction of Jerusalem.
options.note: The opposite; it signals gathering and worship, not destruction.

options.text: It calls Israel's army to war against Egypt.
options.note: Not described; the horn calls exiles home to worship, not to war.

options.text: It marks the beginning of a new famine.
options.note: Unrelated to the text's actual content, which is a joyful ingathering for worship.

questions: The LORD promises to finally slay Leviathan, the ancient serpent. What areas of chaos, fear, or evil in your own life or the world do you need to remember are already under God's certain, coming judgment?

God describes Himself as a keeper who waters His vineyard continually and guards it night and day. Where have you felt distant from that kind of attentive care, and how does this image invite you to trust Him again?

Israel's exile was real discipline, yet distinct from the destruction visited on her oppressors. How do you distinguish, in your own life, between God's loving discipline and simple punishment or abandonment?

True atonement for Jacob's guilt showed itself in the actual removal of idols, not just a change in status. What idols in your own life would need to visibly go for your repentance to bear that same kind of fruit?

Scattered exiles from Assyria and Egypt are gathered by a single ram's horn to worship together. Where do you long to see scattered or estranged people, in your family, church, or community, gathered back into worship and unity?

translationNotes.note: Where BSB prints em dashes around Leviathan the coiling serpent, they have been rendered as commas per house style; the threefold description, fleeing serpent, coiling serpent, dragon of the sea, echoes ancient chaos-monster language used elsewhere of cosmic evil.
Where BSB prints an em dash before yes, let them make peace with Me, it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
Where BSB prints an em dash after lies deserted, it has been rendered as a comma per house style.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 27:1-13. Poetic line breaks joined into running verse text; where BSB prints an em dash it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
